Our new metrics show better alignment with human evaluation. We observe that CLIPScore-Image may give a generated diagram a relatively good score even if its element-wise logic is definitely different from the reference, while CLIPScore-Text is too sensitive to the text description. While our metrics are more aligned to human judgment compared to CLIPScore, the most widely-used metric in the field, we believe they are trustworthy enough to provide another perspective of evaluation to generated diagrams.

### 5.5 Case Study: When and how our metrics and CLIPScore differ

The metric statistics show that our new metrics does not fully consistent with CLIPScore, the classical evaluation metric. This raises a research question: What happen when our metrics and CLIPScore differ? In this section, we select four cases with distinct scores by our metrics and CLIPScore and explain why the difference takes place.

Figure [6](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25761v2#S5.F6 "Figure 6 ‣ 5.3 Statistics ‣ 5 Experiment ‣ DiagramEval: Evaluating LLM-Generated Diagrams via Graphs") shows a case with low CLIPScore (Text) and high Path F1. We can see an explicit data flow in the generated diagram, with good alignment with the semantic of groundtruth diagram as well as the original paper (Wang et al., [2024a](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25761v2#bib.bib18)). This is well captured by our Path F1. However, the spatial layout and icons in the generated diagram are very different from those in the groundtruth. While layout captions used in CLIPScore (Text) include detailed description to these visual elements, the generated diagram performs poorly over CLIPScore (Text). This case consolidates the point that the sensitivity to visual elements of CLIPScore hinder its recognition of good diagrams. More robust to the interference of layouts and icons, our metrics complement this disadvantage.

Figure [6](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25761v2#S5.F6 "Figure 6 ‣ 5.3 Statistics ‣ 5 Experiment ‣ DiagramEval: Evaluating LLM-Generated Diagrams via Graphs") shows a case with high CLIPScore (Text) and low Path F1. In contrast to the above case, there is no clear data flow. Hence, our Path F1 assigns 0 to this diagram. However, the generated diagram includes all text mentioned in the layout caption. For this reason, it has perfect alignment with the layout caption under CLIPScore (Text). This exposes an inherent problem of depending evaluation on models: the generator may hack the metric. Only by placing all text elements mentioned in the layout caption (rather than those existing in the original diagram), the generated diagram yields perfect score in the model-based comparison with the layout caption. Our metrics ease this problem by obtaining intermediates from models.

Figure [8](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25761v2#S5.F8 "Figure 8 ‣ 5.4 Human Evaluation ‣ 5 Experiment ‣ DiagramEval: Evaluating LLM-Generated Diagrams via Graphs") shows a case with low CLIPScore (Image) and high Node F1. Similar to the case in Figure [6](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25761v2#S5.F6 "Figure 6 ‣ 5.3 Statistics ‣ 5 Experiment ‣ DiagramEval: Evaluating LLM-Generated Diagrams via Graphs"), the evaluation of CLIPScore is interfered by the difference in spatial layout and non-textual icons. By contrast, our Node F1 focuses only on the existence of text elements, thus giving a more objective result. Figure [8](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25761v2#S5.F8 "Figure 8 ‣ 5.4 Human Evaluation ‣ 5 Experiment ‣ DiagramEval: Evaluating LLM-Generated Diagrams via Graphs") suffers similar problems, where CLIPScore (Image) ignores detailed logic and evaluates the diagram unreasonably.

To conclude, our metrics overcome two shortcomings of CLIPScore: visual element interference and metric hacking in these four cases. This proves that our metrics constitute good complements to CLIPScore.

## 6 Conclusion

This paper introduces DiagramEval, a novel set of metrics for evaluating large language model (LLM)-generated scientific diagrams. Unlike existing evaluation methods, DiagramEval represents diagrams as graphs and performs automated extraction of graph structures from diagrams. Evaluation metrics are then computed through fine-grained comparisons between the nodes and paths in the generated and reference graphs. DiagramEval addresses the current lack of fine-grained, interpretable, and structure-aware metrics in the assessment of automated diagram generation.

## Limitations

Our proposed metrics have limitations primarily due to uncertainties in LLM performance. Specifically, the LLM may not reliably identify all edges in the reference graph, potentially causing inaccurate or underestimated evaluations. Despite this, our metrics reduce dependency on complex models and offer more interpretable outcomes compared to existing methods. Addressing these limitations is an important direction for future work.

As our work focuses on evaluating diagram generation, it does not raise new potential risks other than those general ones by using LLMs to generate contents.

We sincerely appreciate the support from Amazon grant funding project #120359, "GRAG: Enhance RAG Applications with Graph-structured Knowledge", and Meta gift funding project "PERM: Toward Parameter Efficient Foundation Models for Recommenders".

# Title:DiagramEval: Evaluating LLM-Generated Diagrams via Graphs

Authors:[Chumeng Liang](https://arxiv.org/search/cs?searchtype=author&query=Liang,+C), [Jiaxuan You](https://arxiv.org/search/cs?searchtype=author&query=You,+J)

View a PDF of the paper titled DiagramEval: Evaluating LLM-Generated Diagrams via Graphs, by Chumeng Liang and 1 other authors

[View PDF](/pdf/2510.25761v2) [HTML (experimental)](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.25761v2)

> Abstract:Diagrams play a central role in research papers for conveying ideas, yet they are often notoriously complex and labor-intensive to create. Although diagrams are presented as images, standard image generative models struggle to produce clear diagrams with well-defined structure. We argue that a promising direction is to generate demonstration diagrams directly in textual form as SVGs, which can leverage recent advances in large language models (LLMs). However, due to the complexity of components and the multimodal nature of diagrams, sufficiently discriminative and explainable metrics for evaluating the quality of LLM-generated diagrams remain lacking. In this paper, we propose DiagramEval, a novel evaluation metric designed to assess demonstration diagrams generated by LLMs. Specifically, DiagramEval conceptualizes diagrams as graphs, treating text elements as nodes and their connections as directed edges, and evaluates diagram quality using two new groups of metrics: node alignment and path alignment. For the first time, we effectively evaluate diagrams produced by state-of-the-art LLMs on recent research literature, quantitatively demonstrating the validity of our metrics. Furthermore, we show how the enhanced explainability of our proposed metrics offers valuable insights into the characteristics of LLM-generated diagrams. Code: [this https URL](https://github.com/ulab-uiuc/diagram-eval).

# Title:Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback

Authors:[Aman Madaan](https://arxiv.org/search/cs?searchtype=author&query=Madaan,+A), [Niket Tandon](https://arxiv.org/search/cs?searchtype=author&query=Tandon,+N), [Prakhar Gupta](https://arxiv.org/search/cs?searchtype=author&query=Gupta,+P), [Skyler Hallinan](https://arxiv.org/search/cs?searchtype=author&query=Hallinan,+S), [Luyu Gao](https://arxiv.org/search/cs?searchtype=author&query=Gao,+L), [Sarah Wiegreffe](https://arxiv.org/search/cs?searchtype=author&query=Wiegreffe,+S), [Uri Alon](https://arxiv.org/search/cs?searchtype=author&query=Alon,+U), [Nouha Dziri](https://arxiv.org/search/cs?searchtype=author&query=Dziri,+N), [Shrimai Prabhumoye](https://arxiv.org/search/cs?searchtype=author&query=Prabhumoye,+S), [Yiming Yang](https://arxiv.org/search/cs?searchtype=author&query=Yang,+Y), [Shashank Gupta](https://arxiv.org/search/cs?searchtype=author&query=Gupta,+S), [Bodhisattwa Prasad Majumder](https://arxiv.org/search/cs?searchtype=author&query=Majumder,+B+P), [Katherine Hermann](https://arxiv.org/search/cs?searchtype=author&query=Hermann,+K), [Sean Welleck](https://arxiv.org/search/cs?searchtype=author&query=Welleck,+S), [Amir Yazdanbakhsh](https://arxiv.org/search/cs?searchtype=author&query=Yazdanbakhsh,+A), [Peter Clark](https://arxiv.org/search/cs?searchtype=author&query=Clark,+P)

View a PDF of the paper titled Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback, by Aman Madaan and 15 other authors

[View PDF](/pdf/2303.17651)

> Abstract:Like humans, large language models (LLMs) do not always generate the best output on their first try. Motivated by how humans refine their written text, we introduce Self-Refine, an approach for improving initial outputs from LLMs through iterative feedback and refinement. The main idea is to generate an initial output using an LLMs; then, the same LLMs provides feedback for its output and uses it to refine itself, iteratively. Self-Refine does not require any supervised training data, additional training, or reinforcement learning, and instead uses a single LLM as the generator, refiner, and feedback provider. We evaluate Self-Refine across 7 diverse tasks, ranging from dialog response generation to mathematical reasoning, using state-of-the-art (GPT-3.5, ChatGPT, and GPT-4) LLMs. Across all evaluated tasks, outputs generated with Self-Refine are preferred by humans and automatic metrics over those generated with the same LLM using conventional one-step generation, improving by ~20% absolute on average in task performance. Our work demonstrates that even state-of-the-art LLMs like GPT-4 can be further improved at test time using our simple, standalone approach.

Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback            

# Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback

[Aman Madaan](https://madaan.github.io/)1, [Niket Tandon](https://allenai.org/team/nikett/)2, [Prakhar Gupta](https://prakharguptaz.github.io/)1, [Skyler Hallinan](https://skylerhallinan.com/)3, [Luyu Gao](https://luyug.github.io/)1, [Sarah Wiegreffe](https://sarahwie.github.io/)2, [Uri Alon](https://urialon.ml/)1, [Nouha Dziri](https://webdocs.cs.ualberta.ca/~dziri/)2, [Shrimai Prabhumoye](https://shrimai.github.io/) 4, [Yiming Yang](https://www.cs.cmu.edu/~./yiming/)1, [Sean Welleck](https://wellecks.com/)2,3, [Bodhisattwa Prasad Majumder](https://www.majumderb.com/) 5, [Shashank Gupta](https://shatu.github.io/)2 [Amir Yazdanbakhsh](https://www.ayazdan.com/)6, [Peter Clark](https://allenai.org/team/peterc)2

1Language Technologies Institute, Carnegie Mellon University  
2Allen Institute for AI, 3University of Washington, 4NVIDIA, 5UC San Diego 6Google Research, Brain Team

[Paper](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2303.17651.pdf) [Code](https://github.com/madaan/self-refine) [Demo](https://self-refine-webgen.herokuapp.com/)

Self-Refine is a novel approach that allows LLMs to iteratively refine outputs and incorporate feedback along multiple dimensions to improve performance on diverse tasks. Unlike prior work, it does not require supervised training data or reinforcement learning, and works with a single LLM.

![](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/madaan/self-refine/main/docs/static/images/animation_oldstyle_oneloop.gif)

## Self-Refine iteratively improves outputs from LLMs through a process of iterative creation with feedback description.

---

## Framework Description

  

![](static/images/fig2.png)

Given an input x, and an initial output y0, Self-Refine successively refines the output in a FEEDBACK → REFINE → FEEDBACK loop. It is assumed that the initial output y0 is produced by a generator model, which could be a specialized fine-tuned model or a few-shot prompted model.

  
  

### Components

-   **FEEDBACK**: receives the initial output y0 and provides feedback on how to enhance it. This feedback is task-dependent and generally addresses multiple aspects of the input. In the above example, the feedback might concern the sentiment level and vividness of the review. Importantly, the feedback is actionable, as the feedback module identifies specific areas that can be refined (e.g., “the sentiment level is neutral” or “the sentiment is neutral due to phrases like good”).
  
-   **REFINE** is responsible for refining yt based on the feedback received from the feedback module. In the example, informed by the neutral sentiment of the review due to phrases like “good”, the model may attempt to enhance positivity by substituting “good” with “amazing”.

  
  
  

### Key Features

-   The FEEDBACK → REFINE → FEEDBACK loop can be applied multiple times.
  
-   Self-Refine retains the history of past experiences. This is achieved by appending the previous outputs to the prompt continuously. This allows the system to learn from past mistakes and avoid repeating them
  
-   Self-Refine generates actionable feedback. Given the initial output from an LLM, the FEEDBACK pinpoints the reasons for the output meeting (or not meeting) the requirements. The actionable feedback covers two aspects (i) localization of the problem (ii) instruction to improve.

  
  
  

## Adding a new task

Adding a new task to Self-Refine is straightforward. The following code snippet shows the basic structure of the Self-Refine framework. The FEEDBACK and REFINE modules are task-dependent and can be implemented in a variety of ways. The is\_refinement\_sufficient function defines the stopping criteria for the iterative process. The function is\_refinement\_sufficient is also task-dependent and can be implemented in a variety of ways.

```python

def self_refine(prompt: str) -> str:
    def is_refinement_sufficient(prompt, feedback, initial, refined) -> bool:
        # Define stopping criteria here
        pass

    answer = ChatGPT(prompt)

    while True:
        feedback = ChatGPT(feedback_prompt, answer)
        refined = ChatGPT(refiner_prompt, feedback, answer)

        if is_refinement_sufficient(prompt, feedback, answer, refined):
            break

        answer = refined

    return refined
                        
```

![](static/images/example1.png)

Overview of Self-Refine: given an initial output (left), FEEDBACK evaluates it and generates actionable feedback required to correct it (center). The REFINE takes the feedback into account, and refines the output (right). For example, in the top row, an initial review with negative sentiment is first transformed into a positive one, then further refined through feedback. In the bottom row, an initial code snippet is provided, followed by feedback identifying a more efficient approach, and finally resulting in an optimized code implementation after applying the suggested improvements.

---

## Results summary

## Tasks and Setup

We conduct extensive experiments on 7 diverse tasks of review rewriting, acronym generation, story generation, code rewriting, response generation, constrained generation, and toxicity removal, demonstrating that Self-Refine outperforms direct generation from strong generators like GPT-3.5 and even GPT-4 by at least 5% to more than 40% improvement

  
  

![](static/images/tasks.png)  
The diverse set of tasks to evaluate Self-Refine, along with their associated datasets and sizes. On the right, we show an example of a single iteration of refinement of input from the dataset x, previous generated output yt, feedback generated fb, and refinement produced yt+1 using f

---

## Main results

We show the relative improvements in performance across diverse tasks using the Self-Refine framework. The improvements are measured as the percentage increase in preference rate revealed by human evaluation (“Human Eval.”) or task-specific performance metrics. This demonstrates the effectiveness of the iterative refinement process employed by Self-Refine in generating higher-quality outputs.

![](static/images/mainresults.png) **Self-Refine outperforms the baseline GPT-3 model on all baselines**

---

Impact of Iterative Refinement Self-Refine aims to improve the output iterative following cycles of feedback and refinement. Does the output improve at each step? We analyze this question on three datasets: Sentiment Reversal, Math Reasoning, Code Optimization.

![](static/images/refinement.png)  
**Self-Refine iteratively improves outputs. The rate of Refinement represents the average improvement in percentage points per iteration, calculated from Iteration 0 to Iteration 2**

---

> def solution():

Feedback

> Feedback:
> # Why is this code slow?
> 
> # This code is slow because it is using a brute force approach to find the square root of the input number. It is looping through every possible number starting from 0 until n. Note that the square root will be smaller than n, so at least half of the numbers it is looping through are unnecessary. At most, you need to loop through the numbers up to the square root of n.

> a, b = input().split()

## Concurrent Research and Developments

Iterative refinement with self-feedback is a promising approach to improve large language models (LLMs). Many recent and concurrent works have explored this idea from different perspectives and applications - please checkout the twitter list below. These works are all important and relevant to our research, as they show the potential and challenges of using LLMs as both generators and evaluators of their own outputs.

> In all tasks, outputs generated with SELF-REFINE are preferred by humans and by automated metrics over those generated directly with GPT-3.5 and GPT-4, improving on average by absolute 20% across tasks  
>   
> abs:… [pic.twitter.com/5ZtoerWBN5](https://t.co/5ZtoerWBN5)
> 
> — AK (@\_akhaliq) [April 3, 2023](https://twitter.com/_akhaliq/status/1642697890446340096?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw)

part.text:
# A7 findings

## What the sources actually say

| URL | Concrete technique + exact mechanism |
|---|---|
| https://arxiv.org/html/2508.03560v1 | **LaTCoder / Layout-as-Thought:** split a design into rectangular BBoxes, generate each block independently, then assemble using either absolute-positioned BBoxes or MLLM assembly, with dynamic selection by `0.5 * (1 - MAE/255) + 0.5 * CLIP`. The divider uses OCR to avoid splitting text, 5px grid sampling, min dividing distance `tau=50`, recursive depth 3, and min block area `300x300`. Reported gains include DeepSeek-VL2 on CC-HARD: TreeBLEU `+66.67%`, Visual Score `+12.5%`, MAE `-38.53%`. |
| https://arxiv.org/html/2507.11538v1 | **IFScale / instruction-density cliff:** models lose constraints as instruction count rises. It identifies threshold, linear, and exponential decay, with primacy effects peaking around `150-200` instructions. Practical mechanism: reduce simultaneous constraints, put critical constraints first, and use deterministic checks because failures become omissions under load. |
| https://arxiv.org/html/2509.16891v2 | **LaySPA / spatial reward model:** generate layout JSON with `(x,y,w,h,type)`, then score candidates using format correctness, collision, alignment, distribution, spacing, and semantic underlay-text rewards. Collision is computed as `1 - IoU(overlap)`. Reported Qwen-7B + LaySPA: collision rate reduced from `0.692` to `0.431`, about `-36%`, with alignment `+63%`, spacing `+73%`. |
| https://arxiv.org/html/2605.25447 | **GeoSVG-RL / plan-first + browser geometry verifier:** first emit a layout plan containing canvas, node BBoxes, text assignments, connector endpoints, anchors, and graph connectivity; then generate SVG; then render in browser and compute rewards for render validity, canvas fit, overflow area, anchor accuracy/error, text-in-box, padding violation, edge F1, and code cleanliness. Browser rendering is required for true text metrics. |
| https://github.com/madaan/self-refine | **Self-Refine:** run `GEN -> FEEDBACK -> REFINE` iteratively with the same LLM. Feedback must be actionable, localize the defect, and include improvement instructions. The project exposes three prompt roles: `Init`, `Feedback`, `Iterate`. The paper abstract/source confirms no training/RL required and reports about `+20%` absolute average task improvement. |
| https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/chain-of-rubrics-cor-prompting-framework | **Chain-of-Rubrics:** decompose evaluation into explicit rubric items, run generator/evaluator feedback per rubric, and aggregate weighted rubric checks. The page gives the reward form `sum(w_j * c_j) / sum(w_j)` and cites Rubrics-as-Rewards results up to `28%` relative improvement over Likert baselines. |
| https://docs.z.ai/guides/vlm/glm-4.6v | **GLM multimodal tool loop:** GLM-4.6V supports native multimodal tool calling, visual audit, frontend screenshot-to-code, and visual debugging where a user marks a screenshot region and asks for a code correction. The applicable mechanism is not “more prompt”; it is screenshot/render feedback plus tool-mediated edits. |
| https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.25761 | The listed PDF URL returned raw PDF bytes in `webfetch`, so I used the arXiv HTML fallback for extraction. **DiagramEval:** parse generated SVGs into graph nodes and directed edges, then evaluate Node Alignment and Path Alignment precision/recall/F1. Human correlation: Node F1 `0.4316`, Path F1 `0.4052`, CLIPScore-Text `0.1065`, CLIPScore-Image `0.0831`. |
| https://pub.towardsai.net/stop-fixing-your-ai-svgs-715df70ccca0 | **Computed SVG toolchain:** stop asking LLMs to hand-code coordinates; expose grid, primitives, smart connectors, geometry constraints, callout placement, empty-space detection, charts, and validators. Validation targets overlaps, contrast, alignment, connector integrity, CSS hygiene, and collisions. Concrete algorithms include A* connector routing, Euclidean distance transforms, PCHIP splines, WCAG contrast, greedy constraint satisfaction, and Shapely collision detection. |

## How it maps to this run’s defects

| Priority | Lever | Verdict | Matched RCs | Why it fits this run |
|---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Computed grid + region budgets + smart connectors | **ADOPT** | RC-1, RC-2, RC-3, RC-5, RC-7 | The lowest tiles fail because diagrams claim the whole `480px` canvas, overflow rows, collide nodes, and lose the bottom-title rule. This directly replaces blind coordinate prediction with computed positions and validators. |
| 2 | LaTCoder-style block division and assembly | **ADOPT** | RC-1, RC-2, RC-3 | The run’s diagram tiles score `35-58` while linear tiles reach `86-94`. LaTCoder’s measured gains are specifically on layout preservation and complex layouts. Use fixed bento regions instead of monolithic generation. |
| 3 | GeoSVG-RL verifier metrics without RL training | **ADOPT** | RC-1, RC-2, RC-3 | Browser-backed checks for canvas fit, text-in-box, anchor placement, and edge consistency map directly to overflow, collisions, title placement, and connector drift. |
| 4 | Chain-of-Rubrics audit gate | **ADOPT** | RC-1, RC-2, RC-3, RC-4, RC-5, RC-7 | The failures are rubric-sized: title bottom, no uppercase, no overflow, contrast pass, no collisions. Make each a binary check, not prose. |
| 5 | Self-Refine one-pass repair | **ADOPT** | RC-1, RC-2, RC-4, RC-5 | Use only after render/audit. Feedback must cite concrete failed checks like `visual.bottom=392 > 340` or `contrast=1.51:1 < 4.5:1`. |
| 6 | IFScale prompt-density reduction | **ADAPT** | RC-3, RC-4 | Title-at-bottom and “Title case, not uppercase” are being omitted under dense contracts. Split generation into plan, code, audit, repair. Put non-negotiables first. |
| 7 | LaySPA reward dimensions | **ADAPT** | RC-2, RC-7 | Use its collision/alignment/spacing/distribution rewards as deterministic audit scores. Do not start with RL training. |
| 8 | GLM multimodal tool use | **ADAPT** | RC-1, RC-2, RC-5 | Use GLM’s visual-debug pattern: generate, render screenshot, audit, then repair localized code. Do not trust frontend-replication claims as a standalone fix. |
| 9 | DiagramEval graph scoring | **ADAPT** | RC-2, RC-7 | Useful for flow/diagram correctness, especially missing or wrong edges. It does not replace overflow/contrast checks, so it is a secondary diagram-quality gate. |

## Concrete, testable recommendation

Implement a **layout-plan-first bento pipeline** with deterministic gates before accepting GLM output.

```text
BENTO_LAYOUT_CONTRACT_V1

Canvas:
- Card size: 480x480.
- Outer padding: 24.
- No generated element may render outside [0,0,480,480].

Reserved regions:
- eyebrow: x=24 y=20 w=432 h=24
- visual:  x=24 y=64 w=432 h=276
- title:   x=24 y=368 w=432 h=88

Hard constraints:
- The title block always starts at y >= 368.
- The visual region must end at y <= 340.
- No visual element may overlap the title region.
- Max diagram nodes: 5.
- Max matrix/table rows: 3.
- Max flow edges/connectors: 6.
- Max absolute-positioned elements: 0 unless derived from the declared layout plan.
- Eyebrow text must be Title case, no CSS text-transform uppercase.
- Do not use #4e4e4e or any text color below 4.5:1 contrast on its rendered background.
- Object/line contrast must be >= 3:1.
- Allowed symbolic glyphs are from the approved icon set only; no fallback anchor/move glyphs.

Required output before code:
{
  "canvas": {"w": 480, "h": 480},
  "regions": {
    "eyebrow": [24, 20, 432, 24],
    "visual": [24, 64, 432, 276],
    "title": [24, 368, 432, 88]
  },
  "elements": [
    {"id": "node-1", "type": "card|pill|row|label|connector", "bbox": [x, y, w, h], "region": "visual"}
  ],
  "edges": [
    {"from": "node-1", "to": "node-2", "fromAnchor": "right", "toAnchor": "left"}
  ]
}
```

Add this acceptance gate:

```text
BENTO_AUDIT_EXIT_0

Reject output if any condition is true:
- any bbox x < 0, y < 0, x+w > 480, or y+h > 480
- any visual bbox y+h > 340
- title bbox y < 368
- any visual bbox intersects title bbox
- any pairwise non-connector bbox overlap area > 4px
- any connector endpoint is more than 4px from its declared anchor
- any text contrast < 4.5:1
- any object/line contrast < 3:1
- any CSS contains text-transform: uppercase
- any rendered eyebrow has more than 1 line
- any unapproved glyph appears in eyebrow or hub nodes
```

Run top-3 experiments in this order:

| Experiment | Exact change | Success metric |
|---|---|---|
| A7-E1 | Add `BENTO_LAYOUT_CONTRACT_V1` before generation and require JSON plan before code. | RC-1 overflow count goes from `8` to `0`; all generated titles satisfy `title_at_bottom:true`. |
| A7-E2 | Add deterministic render audit and rerank `n=3` candidates by pass count, with hard reject on overflow, collision, contrast, and title placement. | Contrast audit exits `0`; no node collision in `oci-4`-style flows; diagram tiles improve by at least `+15` score points. |
| A7-E3 | Add one Self-Refine repair pass only for failed audit rows. Feedback must include failed check name and measured value. | At least `50%` of initially failing diagram candidates repaired to audit pass without degrading Product register. |

Recommended dial preset:

```text
Product V4/M2/D6 + Layout Primitive Guard

Generation:
- diagram_mode = constrained-grid
- candidates = 3
- temperature = low
- output_plan_required = true
- prose_contract_max = 12 hard rules

Geometry:
- visual_region = [24,64,432,276]
- title_region = [24,368,432,88]
- max_nodes = 5
- max_rows = 3
- max_edges = 6

Verification:
- render_audit = required
- contrast_gate = WCAG text 4.5:1, object 3:1
- collision_gate = bbox overlap <= 4px
- connector_gate = endpoint <= 4px from anchor
- repair_passes = 1
```

## Predicted effect + cost

| Change | Predicted SHIP-rate effect | Diagram-vs-linear delta | Contrast exit-0 | Cost |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| Region contract + computed grid | `+4` to `+6` shipped tiles, from `27/45` to roughly `31-33/45` | Low diagram tiles move from `35-58` toward `70-82` | Neutral | `+300-600` prompt tokens, no extra model call |
| Render audit + `n=3` rerank | Additional `+2` to `+4` shipped tiles | Catches overflow/collision before scoring | High, if palette enforced | About `3x` generation latency plus browser audit |
| One Self-Refine repair pass | Additional `+1` to `+3` shipped tiles | Repairs residual layout and uppercase/glyph failures | High, if audit feedback is numeric | `+1` model call only for failed candidates |
| Chain-of-Rubrics checklist | Stabilizes gains, reduces regressions | Turns vague design quality into binary gates | High | `+200-400` tokens |
| DiagramEval-style graph check | Better flow correctness, less slop | Improves diagram semantics, not canvas fit | Neutral | Low if DOM/SVG parsed; higher if VLM edge extraction is used |

Expected combined effect: `34-37/45` SHIP, or roughly `76-82%`, if the top three changes are implemented together and the verifier is a hard gate. This is a prediction, not a confirmed result.

## Open questions for the next iteration

- What is the exact title/description block height in the current scoring rubric: is `88px` enough, or should the reserved bottom region be `104-112px`?

- Are the generated tiles SVG, HTML/CSS, or React DOM at audit time? This determines whether DiagramEval-style graph extraction can be deterministic or needs a VLM.

- Should low-scoring 2D diagrams be converted to linear-flow variants by default when they exceed `5` nodes or `3` rows?

- Which brand palette tokens replace `#4e4e4e` while preserving Product register V4/M2/D6?

- Does GLM-5.2 support native tool/image feedback like GLM-4.6V, or does the render/audit loop need to be external-only?
